question_text: Capital Fund in the Balance Sheet of an NPO is equivalent to:
explanation: Capital Fund (also called General Fund) is equivalent to Capital/Owner's Equity in a business. It represents the excess of assets over liabilities. It is increased by surplus and life membership fees, and decreased by deficit.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Capital / Owner's Equity in a business

elimination_hints.hint: Capital Fund is the owners' (members') stake, not a liability.
Reserve Fund is a specific reserve. Capital Fund is the total net worth.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing specific reserve with total fund.

elimination_hints.hint: P&L balance is for businesses. NPOs have Capital Fund.

question_text: Capital Fund is calculated as:
explanation: Capital Fund = Total Assets – Total External Liabilities. This is the accounting equation applied to NPOs. It represents the net worth of the organisation belonging to its members.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This is the inverse. Capital Fund = Assets – Liabilities, not the other way.
Income – Expenditure gives Surplus/Deficit, not Capital Fund.
Receipts – Payments gives closing cash balance, not Capital Fund.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cash position with net worth.

question_text: Assets: Building ₹10,00,000; Furniture ₹2,00,000; Investments ₹5,00,000; Cash ₹50,000; Outstanding Subscriptions ₹30,000. Liabilities: Creditors ₹80,000; Outstanding Expenses ₹40,000. The opening Capital Fund is:
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹17,80,000 is Total Assets. Capital Fund = Assets – Liabilities.
Check: include ALL assets including cash and outstanding subs.
₹18L doesn't match any calculation. Assets = ₹17.8L, Liabilities = ₹1.2L.

question_text: Surplus from Income & Expenditure Account is:
explanation: Surplus (excess of income over expenditure) is ADDED to the Capital Fund on the liabilities side of the Balance Sheet. It increases the net worth of the organisation. Deficit is deducted from Capital Fund.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Added to Capital Fund on liabilities side of Balance Sheet
Shown as an asset on the Balance Sheet
Transferred to Receipts and Payments Account

elimination_hints.hint: Surplus increases the fund (liability side), not the asset side.
NPOs do not distribute dividends — surplus stays in the Capital Fund.
R&P is a cash summary — surplus from I&E goes to Balance Sheet.

question_text: Statement: Outstanding subscriptions at the end of the year are shown as an asset on the Balance Sheet of an NPO.
explanation: This is TRUE. Outstanding subscriptions represent amounts due from members but not yet received. They are current assets (receivables) and appear on the assets side of the Balance Sheet. They are also added to subscription income in the I&E Account.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Outstanding subs = money owed TO the NPO = receivable = asset.

question_text: Subscriptions received in advance are shown in the Balance Sheet as:
explanation: Subscriptions received in advance (for the next year) are a LIABILITY because the NPO has received money but the service (membership) for that period is yet to be provided. They appear on the liabilities side of the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Deducted from subscriptions on asset side

elimination_hints.hint: Advance received = obligation to provide future service = liability, not asset.
Advance subs are temporary obligations, not part of permanent Capital Fund.
It is shown as a separate liability, not netted against assets.

question_text: Opening Capital Fund ₹15,00,000. Surplus for the year ₹2,50,000. Life Membership Fees received ₹75,000. Deficit from specific fund ₹20,000. The closing Capital Fund is:
explanation: Closing Capital Fund = Opening + Surplus + Life Membership Fees = ₹15,00,000 + ₹2,50,000 + ₹75,000 = ₹18,25,000. Deficit from a specific fund does NOT reduce Capital Fund — it reduces that specific fund.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹18,05K = ₹15L + ₹2.5L + ₹75K – ₹20K. Specific fund deficit doesn't affect Capital Fund.
elimination_hints.misconception: Deducting specific fund deficit from Capital Fund.

elimination_hints.hint: You may have excluded Life Membership Fees. It is added to Capital Fund.
elimination_hints.misconception: Excluding life membership from capital fund.

elimination_hints.hint: Check: ₹15L + ₹2.5L + ₹75K = ₹18,25,000. No extra addition needed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Adding the specific fund deficit instead of ignoring it.

question_text: Specific funds like Building Fund, Tournament Fund are shown on the Balance Sheet as:
explanation: Specific funds are shown separately on the LIABILITIES side of the Balance Sheet. They are earmarked for specific purposes and are not part of the general Capital Fund. Their related investments (if any) are shown on the assets side.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Specific funds are separate from Capital Fund — they have earmarked purposes.
Funds are shown on liabilities side. Their investments may be on assets side.
Specific funds bypass I&E Account — they go directly to Balance Sheet.

question_text: Stock of stationery at the beginning ₹8,000. Stationery purchased during the year ₹45,000. Stock of stationery at the end ₹12,000. Stationery consumed (expense for I&E) is:
explanation: Stationery consumed = Opening stock + Purchases – Closing stock = ₹8,000 + ₹45,000 – ₹12,000 = ₹41,000. The closing stock (₹12,000) appears as an asset on the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹45,000 is amount purchased. Consumed = Opening + Purchased – Closing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using purchase as expense without stock adjustment.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹53K = ₹8K + ₹45K. You added opening stock but forgot to deduct closing stock.
₹37K = ₹45K – ₹8K. Opening stock should be added, not deducted.

question_text:
Assertion (A): The Balance Sheet of an NPO shows Capital Fund on the liabilities side even though there are no owners.
Reason (R): Capital Fund represents the accumulated net worth belonging to the organisation and its members, serving the same function as owner's equity.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Although NPOs have no 'owners' in the business sense, the Capital Fund represents the collective wealth of the members/organisation. It serves the same accounting function as owner's equity.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY Capital Fund appears on liabilities side — it's the net worth.
R is factually true — Capital Fund does represent accumulated net worth.
A is correct — Capital Fund IS shown on the liabilities side of NPO Balance Sheet.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking Capital Fund is shown elsewhere.

question_text: Prepaid subscription at the end of the year is shown in the Balance Sheet as:
explanation: Prepaid subscription (subscription paid in advance by members for next year) is a LIABILITY for the NPO because it has received money for a service not yet provided. It appears on the liabilities side of the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Advance received by NPO = obligation = liability, not asset.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing advance received with advance paid.

elimination_hints.hint: Advance subscription is a B/S item, not an expense.
It is a temporary liability, not a permanent part of Capital Fund.

question_text: Which of the following is the correct order for preparing NPO financial statements?
explanation: The correct order is: (1) Prepare opening Balance Sheet (to find Capital Fund), (2) Prepare R&P Account (summary of cash transactions), (3) Prepare I&E Account (adjustments for accrual), (4) Prepare closing Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

options.text: R&P → I&E → Opening B/S → Closing B/S
I&E → R&P → Closing B/S → Opening B/S
Opening B/S → I&E → R&P → Closing B/S
Opening B/S → R&P → I&E → Closing B/S

elimination_hints.hint: Opening B/S must come first to determine opening Capital Fund.
I&E cannot be prepared before R&P — R&P provides the base data for adjustments.
R&P must come before I&E because I&E adjustments are based on R&P data.

question_text: The Balance Sheet of an NPO prepared at the beginning of the year using given assets and liabilities is called the ______ Balance Sheet.
explanation: The Opening Balance Sheet is prepared at the beginning of the accounting year using the given list of assets and liabilities. Its primary purpose is to determine the opening Capital Fund (Assets – Liabilities).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Closing Balance Sheet is prepared at the END of the year.
Trial Balance is a verification tool, not a Balance Sheet type.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing trial balance with balance sheet.

elimination_hints.hint: Interim statements are mid-year reports, not the standard opening B/S.

question_text: Prize Fund Investments ₹2,00,000 appear on the Balance Sheet. Interest on Prize Fund Investments ₹16,000 was received. Prizes awarded ₹25,000. What is the Prize Fund balance on closing B/S?
explanation: Prize Fund (opening assumed as investments value) = ₹2,00,000. Add: Interest received ₹16,000. Less: Prizes awarded ₹25,000. Closing Prize Fund = ₹2,00,000 + ₹16,000 – ₹25,000 = ₹1,91,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹2,16K = ₹2L + ₹16K. You forgot to deduct prizes awarded.
₹1,75K = ₹2L – ₹25K. You forgot to add interest earned.
The fund changes with interest income and prize expenses.

question_text: Statement: An NPO does not prepare a Trading Account because it does not engage in buying and selling of goods.
explanation: This is TRUE. NPOs do not trade (buy and sell goods for profit). Therefore, they do not prepare a Trading Account. Instead, they prepare Receipts & Payments Account, Income & Expenditure Account, and Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: NPOs provide services, not trade goods. No buying/selling = no Trading Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking NPOs need a Trading Account.

question_text: Investments of an NPO (general investments, not specific fund investments) are shown on the Balance Sheet as:
explanation: General investments of an NPO are shown on the ASSETS side of the Balance Sheet under Non-Current Assets (or Investments). The interest earned on these investments is shown as income in the I&E Account.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Non-Current Asset on the assets side

elimination_hints.hint: Investments are owned resources — assets, not liabilities.
The investment itself is an asset. Interest on it is income.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the investment with its return.

elimination_hints.hint: Capital Fund is on the liabilities side. Investments are on assets side.

question_text: If an NPO has a deficit (expenditure > income), its effect on the Balance Sheet is:
explanation: A deficit reduces the Capital Fund on the Balance Sheet. Closing Capital Fund = Opening Capital Fund – Deficit. This means the organisation's net worth has decreased during the year.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Deficit = expenditure > income. This REDUCES the fund, not increases.
Deficit does not increase assets — it reduces the net worth.
Deficit always affects B/S by reducing Capital Fund.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking I&E result has no B/S impact.

question_text: Furniture of a club was valued at ₹3,00,000 at the beginning. Depreciation charged 10%. New furniture purchased ₹50,000. Closing value of furniture on B/S is:
explanation: Depreciation = 10% × ₹3,00,000 = ₹30,000. Closing value = Opening – Depreciation + Purchase = ₹3,00,000 – ₹30,000 + ₹50,000 = ₹3,20,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹2,70K = ₹3L – ₹30K. You forgot to add the new purchase.
₹3,50K = ₹3L + ₹50K. You forgot to deduct depreciation.
₹3L is the opening value. Both depreciation and purchase affect it.

question_text: Which of the following is a liability on the Balance Sheet of an NPO?
explanation: Outstanding salaries is a liability — it represents wages earned by staff but not yet paid. The NPO owes this amount. Cash in hand, building, and investments are all assets.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Cash in hand is a current asset, not a liability.
Building is a fixed asset.
Investments are assets (resources owned).

question_text:
Match the NPO items with their Balance Sheet presentation:

Column I:
(i) Outstanding Subscriptions
(ii) Advance Subscriptions
(iii) Capital Fund
(iv) Building Fund Donations

Column II:
(P) Liability — specific fund
(Q) Asset — current
(R) Liability — members' fund
(S) Liability — income received in advance
explanation: Outstanding Subscriptions → Asset (Q) — money owed to NPO. Advance Subscriptions → Liability — income received in advance (S). Capital Fund → Liability — members' fund (R). Building Fund Donations → Liability — specific fund (P).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Outstanding subs are receivable (asset Q), not advance received (S).
Advance subs is income received in advance (S), not specific fund (P).
Capital Fund is members' fund (R), not asset (Q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Placing Capital Fund on asset side.
